spec: Evaluate the proportional presence of the cat in the image. Determine how much of the image's total area the cat occupies, and whether that occupation feels substantial and complete. A cat that fills most of the frame scores highly, while a cat reduced to a small speck in a vast scene scores low. Critically, this evaluation must account for completeness and wholeness: a cat whose full body — or at minimum its full face and upper body — is generously contained within the frame is more dominant than one so aggressively cropped that ears, paws, or tail are clipped away, leaving the viewer with the sense of a fragmented subject rather than a commanding one. The ideal proportional presence is a cat that fills the frame significantly with its whole form, feeling large in visual real estate without feeling incomplete. Score closer to 1 when the cat occupies a large share of the frame while appearing whole and intact. Score closer to 0 when the cat is tiny, distant, or so heavily cropped that it feels fragmented rather than present.

Evaluate the positional authority of the cat in the image. Determine where the cat is located within the frame and whether that placement conveys visual importance and compositional intention. A cat placed at or near the center of the image, or positioned along the intersecting lines of thirds — the natural focal lines used in photographic composition — carries strong positional authority. A cat pushed to the extreme edges or corners of the frame, as if the photographer barely caught it before it walked out of the shot, feels marginalized, incidental, and accidental. Consider whether the cat's placement feels deliberate and compositionally purposeful: a centered cat in a balanced composition, or a cat sitting confidently on a rule-of-thirds intersection with balanced space around it, radiates dominance. Score closer to 1 when the cat occupies a position of natural visual power — center, near-center, or a strong compositional focal point — conveying intention and importance. Score closer to 0 when the cat is pushed to the margins, tucked into corners, or placed in a way that feels accidental and compositionally weak.

Evaluate the subject isolation of the cat in the image. Determine how cleanly the cat separates from its surroundings and how free the image is from competing visual elements that pull the viewer's attention away from the cat. A cat photographed against a smooth, simple, uncluttered background achieves high subject isolation — there is nothing else for the eye to latch onto, so the cat becomes the only possible subject. A cat surrounded by a chaotic, busy environment — patterned furniture, scattered objects, bright competing colors, other animals, text overlays, or other visual noise — must fight for the viewer's attention, and its dominance is diluted even if it is large and centrally placed. Consider whether the visual environment surrenders attention to the cat or forces the cat to compete for it. Score closer to 1 when the background is clean, quiet, and free of distracting elements, allowing the cat to stand forward as the singular, unambiguous focus. Score closer to 0 when the scene is visually noisy, cluttered, or filled with competing elements that draw the eye away from the cat.
